sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Termination of resuscitation (TOR) and recognition of life extinct (ROLE) require structured clinical assessment, awareness of local law and policy, and careful team decision-making.

- Do not commence or continue resuscitation if doing so is unsafe for the team.
- Do not commence resuscitation when there are unequivocal signs of death or injuries incompatible with life, in keeping with local policy and scope of practice.
- Where available, check for a valid advance care directive, acute resuscitation plan, DNAR/NFR order, or other legally recognised direction.
- If the situation is unclear, or there is uncertainty about eligibility for ROLE/TOR, commence or continue resuscitation while obtaining more information.
- Use validated TOR rules and service policy to guide decisions, but do not rely on a single rule in isolation.
- Consider the full clinical picture, including witnessed status, initial rhythm, bystander CPR/defibrillation, response to treatment, ETCO2 trends, recurrent or refractory VF, intermittent ROSC, comorbidity, frailty, transport time, and likely reversible causes.
- Maintain a low threshold for consultation with the on-call doctor or RFDS, especially in young patients, pregnancy, overdose, asthma, drowning, hypothermia, persistent shockable rhythm, traumatic arrest, or possible pseudo-PEA.
- Any patient in PEA with a narrow complex and/or rate of 40 bpm or greater requires senior consultation before TOR.
- Document the clinical findings, reasoning, consultation, and team discussion clearly and completely.

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Residual electrical activity or pacemaker spikes may occasionally be seen after effective circulation has ceased. Do not rely on these findings alone; interpret them in the full clinical context and local ROLE/TOR criteria.
- Do not simply wait for deterioration to asystole if the overall picture remains uncertain. Reassess for reversible causes, signs of output, and the need for senior consultation.
- Explain events and decisions to family or other necessary parties with empathy, honesty, and clarity, and provide appropriate support.
